request.question: What is the nationality of this professional boxer who lost to Breidis Prescott in a upset match in 2008 that was heavily favoured toward him?

title: 1929–30 NHL season
Daulis Prescott
1954 FIFA World Cup Final
Breidis Prescott
1993 Hamilton Tiger-Cats season
Jorge Rubio (boxing trainer)
104th Grey Cup
Amir Khan (boxer)
Federico Gaio
1982–83 Quebec Nordiques season
sentences: The 1929–30 NHL season was the 13th season of the National Hockey League.
Ten teams played 44 games each.
The Montreal Canadiens upset the heavily favoured Boston Bruins two games to none for the Stanley Cup.
Daulis Prescott Consuegra (born 2 September 1986) is a Colombian professional boxer who has challenged once for the WBA featherweight title in 2012.
His brother Breidis Prescott is also a professional boxer.
The 1954 FIFA World Cup Final was the final match of the 1954 FIFA World Cup, the fifth World Cup in FIFA history.
The game was played at the Wankdorf Stadium in Bern, Switzerland, on 4 July 1954, and saw West Germany beat the heavily favoured Golden Team of Hungary 3–2.
Breidis Enrique Prescott Consuegra (born May 3, 1983), best known as Breidis Prescott, is a Colombian professional boxer.
In his early career he had a reputation as a fearsome puncher, winning 18 of his first 20 fights by knockout.
In 2008, Prescott first became known on the world stage when he upset heavily favoured and then-undefeated Olympian Amir Khan, which gave rise to Prescott's nickname of "The Khanqueror".
The 1993 Hamilton Tiger-Cats season was the 36th season for the team in the Canadian Football League and their 44th overall.
The Tiger-Cats finished in 2nd place in the East division with a 6–12–0 record.
After defeating the Ottawa Rough Riders in the East Semi-Final, the team appeared in the East Final, but lost to the heavily favoured Winnipeg Blue Bombers by a single point.
Jorge Enrique Rubio is a Cuban trainer in the sport of boxing.
Rubio was a trainer of the Cuban national amateur boxing team.
Rubio trains numerous professional fighters in Miami, Florida, including Gil Reyes, Juan Arroyo, and Stacey Reile.
Rubio was announced as Amir Khan's trainer on July 26, 2008.
After Khan's disastrous first-round KO defeat at the hands of relatively unknown Colombian Breidis Prescott, Khan and Rubio parted ways.

The 104th Grey Cup (branded as the 104th Grey Cup presented by Shaw for sponsorship reasons) was a Canadian football game that was played on November 27, 2016, between the Calgary Stampeders and the Ottawa Redblacks, that decided the champion for the 2016 CFL season.
In an upset, Ottawa defeated the heavily favoured Stampeders 39–33 in overtime to win a championship in just their third season of existence.
This was the third Grey Cup game to go into overtime (the other two instances came in 1961 and 2005).

This also marked a first that a CFL team won its division with a losing record (8-9-1) and became the 3rd worst team (behind the 2000 BC Lions and 2001 Calgary Stampeders who both went 8-10) to win the Grey Cup.
Amir Iqbal Khan (born 8 December 1986) is a British professional boxer.
He is a former unified light-welterweight world champion, having held the WBA (later Super) title from 2009 to 2012, and the IBF title in 2011.
Additionally he held the Commonwealth lightweight title from 2007 to 2008, the WBC Silver welterweight title from 2014 to 2016, and has challenged once for a middleweight world title in 2016.
Federico Gaio (born 5 March 1992 in Faenza, Italy) is an Italian professional tennis player.
He competes mainly in the Challenger Tour and ITF circuit.
He has seven ITF singles titles and three ITF doubles titles.

He reached his career high singles ranking of World No. 158 in August 2016 after winning his maiden singles challenger title, defeating the heavily favoured Tomasz Bellucci 76, 62 at the Biella Challenger in Italy
The 1982–83 Quebec Nordiques season was the Nordiques fourth season in the National Hockey League.

In the 1981–82 season, Quebec recorded their first winning season in the NHL, as they had a record of 33-31-16, earning 82 points, in which they finished fourth in the Adams Division, qualifying for the post-season for the second consecutive season.

The Nordiques upset the heavily favoured Montreal Canadiens and Boston Bruins to represent the Adams Division in the Wales Conference finals, however, the New York Islanders swept Quebec in the Conference finals, preventing the club from making the Stanley Cup finals.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What is the nationality of this professional boxer who lost to Breidis Prescott in a upset match in 2008 that was heavily favoured toward him?
hop1_docs: Breidis Prescott | Breidis Enrique Prescott Consuegra (born May 3, 1983), best known as Breidis Prescott, is a Colombian professional boxer.  In his early career he had a reputation as a fearsome puncher, winning 18 of his first 20 fights by knockout.  In 2008, Prescott first became known on the world stage when he upset heavily favoured and then-undefeated Olympian Amir Khan, which gave rise to Prescott's nickname of "The Khanqueror".

Daulis Prescott | Daulis Prescott Consuegra (born 2 September 1986) is a Colombian professional boxer who has challenged once for the WBA featherweight title in 2012.  His brother Breidis Prescott is also a professional boxer.

Jorge Rubio (boxing trainer) | Jorge Enrique Rubio is a Cuban trainer in the sport of boxing.  Rubio was a trainer of the Cuban national amateur boxing team.  Rubio trains numerous professional fighters in Miami, Florida, including Gil Reyes, Juan Arroyo, and Stacey Reile.  Rubio was announced as Amir Khan's trainer on July 26, 2008.  After Khan's disastrous first-round KO defeat at the hands of relatively unknown Colombian Breidis Prescott, Khan and Rubio parted ways.

Charles Manyuchi | Charles Manyuchi is a Zimbabwean professional boxer who is the WBC, international welterweight champion since March 2014.  In November 2014, he successfully defended his WBC crown in a Lusaka, Zambia fight against an opponent from Colombia.  In 2014 he was awarded the Zimbabwe's Sportsperson of the year accolade.  In July 2015 against an Italian boxer in Friuli-Venezia Giulia, he defended his crown successfully.In 2017 saw the fall of great man who was bound for glory and wider recognition internationally when he lost his title to Quadratilo from Singapore in what was referred as a huge upset in the boxing world especially to the fans back home.  Coming back home,he received unwelcome remarks and comments from many fans but he however had support from government claiming to sue those who continued to attack him.  The are currently plans to have a rematch.

Leon Spinks | Leon Spinks (born July 11, 1953) is an American former professional boxer who competed from 1977 to 1995.  In only his eighth professional fight, he won the undisputed heavyweight championship in 1978 after defeating Muhammad Ali via split decision, in what was considered one of the biggest upsets in boxing history.  Spinks was later stripped of the WBC title for facing Ali in an unapproved rematch seven months later, which he lost by unanimous decision.

Samuel Peter | Samuel Okon Peter (born September 6, 1980) is a Nigerian-American professional boxer who held the WBC heavyweight title in 2008.  He rose to prominence in his early career following a string of knockout wins, at a time when the titles of the heavyweight division were largely fragmented.  In March 2008, Peter won a portion of the world heavyweight championship by knocking out Oleg Maskaev to win the WBC title.  In October, in what would be his only defense of the title, Peter lost to a returning Vitali Klitschko, who stopped him in eight rounds.  Peter is known for his rivalry with the Klitschko brothers, having faced Wladimir twice (in 2005 and 2010) and Vitali once.

1929–30 NHL season | The 1929–30 NHL season was the 13th season of the National Hockey League.  Ten teams played 44 games each.  The Montreal Canadiens upset the heavily favoured Boston Bruins two games to none for the Stanley Cup.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Breidis Prescott is described as having "upset heavily favoured and then-undefeated Olympian Amir Khan" (quote) — see passage [1] ("Breidis Prescott").
  - The defeat is corroborated: "Khan's disastrous first-round KO defeat" (quote) — see passage [3] ("Jorge Rubio (boxing trainer)").
- Ambiguities:
  - Only one opponent is named in the passages: "Amir Khan". The passages do not include an explicit page title for Amir Khan (boxer) or state his nationality. No alternate Amir Khan variants/pages appear in the supplied passages.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as written in the passages): "Amir Khan".
  - Missing fact needed to answer the question: Amir Khan's nationality (e.g., the lede/infobox nationality or country he represents).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "Amir Khan (boxer)" page — check the lede/infobox and "Early life" or first paragraph for nationality. Example search: "Amir Khan nationality lede infobox".
  2) If multiple results, open "Amir Khan (disambiguation)" to select the boxer page.
  3) Re-open "Breidis Prescott" page and check the "2008" / "Professional career" section to confirm the match context and opponent spelling if needed.
  4) Optionally check reliable boxing-summary sections (e.g., career overview) on the Amir Khan page for country/representation details.
Final answer deferred until those pages and sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: Amir Amanullah Khan Award | The Amir Amanullah Khan Award is the highest civilian award of Afghanistan.  It was instituted in 2006 and is bestowed by the Government of Afghanistan to Afghan and foreign nationals in recognition of their services to Afghanistan.  The award is named for Amanullah Khan, the Sovereign of the Kingdom of Afghanistan from 1919 to 1929.  The citation on the reverse of the medal states, ""nishan-e dawlati" Ghazi Amir Amanullah Khan", meaning "State Order of Ghazi Amir Amanullah Khan."
Amir Khan Sabri | Hajji Amir Khan Sabri was elected to represent Khost Province in Afghanistan's Wolesi Jirga, the lower house of its National Legislature, in 2005.
Qaleh-ye Amir Khan | Qaleh-ye Amir Khan or Qaleh Amir Khan (Persian: قلعه امير خان‎ ‎ ) may refer to:

Khalid Amir Khan | Khalid Amir Khan is a Pakistani diplomat and politician.  Born on 2 December 1934 to Bilquis Amir and Amir Abdullah Khan, a large landlord from a prominent Pashtun family settled in Sargodha District, Punjab, Pakistan.  Khalid Amir served as ambassador to Hungary, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan in the 1990s, and played a major role in the politics of the Punjab and especially the Sargodha district.

Amir Khan I | Mir Amir Khan I, Amir Khan I was the title of Mir Abul Wafa the eldest son of Mir Qasim Khan Namkiu.  He was a nobleman in the time of the Mughal emperors Jahangir and Shah Jahan.  At the time of his death he was Subedar or governor of Thatta where he died 1647 aged more than 100 years.

Amir Khan II | Amir Khan II surnamed Mir Miran the son of Khalilullah Khan Yezdi was a nobleman of high rank in the time of the Mughal emperors Shah Jahan and Alamgir and a great favorite of the latter.  He was Subedar or governor of Kabul.  He died at Kabul on the April 28, 1698 and the emperor conferred the title of Amir Khan III on his son.
Khan Amir | Khan Amir (Persian: خان امير‎ ‎ ) may refer to:
response.summary_2: The passages do not specify the nationality of Amir Khan, the boxer who lost to Breidis Prescott. Amir Amanullah Khan Award is the highest civilian award of Afghanistan. Amir Khan Sabri was elected to represent Khost Province in Afghanistan's Wolesi Jirga in 2005.
